citation.proposition: A hirer of an independent contractor is presumptively not liable for injuries sustained by the contractor's employees while performing the contracted work.

citations.reasoning: Privette v. Superior Court is the seminal California Supreme Court case establishing the 'Privette doctrine,' which generally shields hirers from liability for injuries to independent contractors' employees.
citations.confidence_reasoning: This is a foundational case in California tort law.

citation.proposition: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

citations.reasoning: The proposition uses the word 'never,' which is legally inaccurate. The Privette doctrine has well-established exceptions, such as the 'retained control' doctrine (Hooker v. Department of Transportation) and the 'nondelegable duty' doctrine (Kinsman v. Unocal Corp).
citations.confidence_reasoning: The Privette doctrine is a presumptive rule, not an absolute immunity.

citation.text: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004)
citation.proposition: Granting summary judgment to hirer where subcontractor controlled scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade.

citations.reasoning: A search for 'Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co.' at 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189 does not yield a matching case. The citation appears to be a hallucination or a misidentification of a different case.
citations.confidence_reasoning: Standard legal databases do not contain this case name at this citation.

citation.text: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991)
citation.case_name: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
citation.proposition: Where an employer demonstrates full compliance with applicable OSHA standards, it is entitled to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.

citations.reasoning: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc. at 887 F.2d 1204 does not exist. Furthermore, the proposition regarding a 'rebuttable presumption' for OSHA compliance is not a standard rule of federal negligence law in the 9th Circuit.
citations.confidence_reasoning: The citation format and the legal proposition do not align with known 9th Circuit jurisprudence.

citation.text: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011)
citation.case_name: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc.
citation.proposition: Compliance with statutory safety requirements is highly probative of the exercise of due care.

citations.reasoning: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc. (2011) 52 Cal.4th 590 actually holds that a hirer is not liable for injuries to a contractor's employee even if the hirer failed to comply with safety regulations, unless the hirer affirmatively contributed to the injury.
citations.confidence_reasoning: This case is a major follow-up to Privette and explicitly rejects the idea that regulatory compliance is the primary test for liability.

facts.claim: On or about March 14, 2021, Rivera was working on a scaffolding assembly at the project site when a section of the scaffolding gave way.
facts.contradiction: The incident occurred on March 12, 2021, not March 14, 2021.
facts.confidence_reasoning: The date of the incident is a verifiable objective fact documented in the official police report and medical records.

facts.claim: Rivera was not wearing required personal protective equipment at the time of the incident, including fall-arrest equipment mandated by site safety protocols.
facts.contradiction: The police report and witness statement confirm Rivera was wearing his required safety harness and hard hat at the time of the collapse.
facts.source_quote: Site supervisor Mark Ellison confirmed that Rivera was wearing a hard hat and harness consistent with site requirements at the time of the collapse.
facts.confidence_reasoning: The police report explicitly documents that the site supervisor confirmed the use of PPE, directly contradicting the MSJ's claim.

facts.claim: The incident giving rise to this action occurred on March 14, 2021.
facts.confidence_reasoning: The date of the incident is a verifiable objective fact documented in the official police report and medical records.
